prompt: Where in the corpus is a famous philosopher-jurist's climate theory discredited by pointing out that he derived it from an experiment performed on half a sheep's tongue — alternately heating it and freezing it and inspecting the fibers under a magnifying glass — and where the author concludes that 'a moral and political system that rested on such experiments would seem to me to rest on a scarcely solid base'? Quote the passage that describes the half-tongue experiment, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The half-a-sheep's-tongue experiment as the empirical basis of climate theory is a uniquely vivid factoid; the authorial sneer at it is verbatim-quotable and not present in any other corpus work, including Comte's other volumes which do not treat Montesquieu's climate system at this length.

prompt: Which work in the corpus argues that European colonization in America, southern Asia, the islands of the great Ocean, and southern and western Africa has produced a wholly new kind of social ordering that previous quarrels over rank cannot describe — naming it 'the aristocracy of species' and predicting that it will considerably influence the existence of the American republics? Quote the line that names this new aristocracy and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The phrase 'aristocracy of species' (l'aristocratie d'espèce) framed as a brand-new category emerging from colonization is a distinctive coinage. The slavery volumes (Vols III–IV) treat slavery directly but do not introduce this comparative-races/perfectibility framing; the property treatises and other corpus works are wholly off-topic.

prompt: Where in the corpus are the inhabitants of Tierra del Fuego and the neighboring Patagonians said to have a complexion approaching 'that of iron rust mixed with oil', adduced as evidence — together with their copper-colored kin running all the way to the Saint Lawrence and Bering Strait across one and a half million square leagues — that complexion does not vary with latitude or temperature? Quote the iron-rust-and-oil line and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The 'iron rust mixed with oil' simile applied to Patagonians, paired with the specific 1.5-million-square-leagues span from Tierra del Fuego to the Bering Strait, is a uniquely concrete factoid. The slavery volumes do not survey the American copper-colored peoples this way, and the climate framing is specific to this volume.
